item.title: How to Access Your Router’s Web Interface Remotely Using SSH Tunneling
item.author: Jaecom
item.content:encoded: Securely connect to your home router from a remote machine using an SSH tunnel — ideal for port forwarding, remote network access, and router configuration.Hello everyone, I recently had to access my router’s web interface to add some port forwarding rules for my Raspberry Pi. The challenge? My Raspberry Pi and router sits in my house — far from where I currently live. Here’s what I had:SSH access to my Raspberry PiThe Raspberry Pi was connected to the router whose port forwarding rules I needed to changeI knew my home router’s administrative passwordThe solution was simple — create an to access the router’s web interface using the Raspberry Pi from my MacBook!ssh -i ~/.ssh/id_rsa \ -p 22 \ -L 8888:192.168.219.1:80 \ pi@xxx.xxx.xxx.xx-i: Identity file — used to SSH into my Raspberry Pi-p: Port — port number to connect SSH (defaults to 22)-L: Local-port Forwardingcreates a SSH tunnel to a specified addresspi@xxx.xxx.xxx.xx: IP address of my Raspberry PI to connect via SSHThis command connects your terminal to the host like a regular SSH session. However, a SSH web tunnel will also be active in the background as long as you don’t exit the session.Now, opening localhost:8888 provides me access to the router web interface directly from my MacBook:Let’s take a close look at this part:8888: Local port that opens on 192.168.219.1: Internal IP of the router, as seen from the Raspberry Pi80: The HTTP port the router’s web interface usesTraffic Flow (What Actually Happens)How does traffic actually flow? Here are the steps that occurs after the command is run:I open http://localhost:8888.2. The SSH Daemon on my MacBook accepts that TCP connection because of -L 8888:.3. The data is encrypted and sent through the SSH tunnel to my Raspberry Pi (pi@xxx.xxx.xxx.xx).4. My Raspberry Pi decrypts it and initiates a to 192.168.219.1:80 from its own network.5. Data travel back and forth between my MacBook(localhost:8888) and (192.168.219.1:80) giving me access to my router web interface!Here’s the fully explicit version of the forwarding flag:-L 127.0.0.1:8888:192.168.219.1:80I think this is easier to understand at first glance, since 127.0.0.1(localhost):8888 on my host machine is being tunneled into 192.168.219.1:80 on my remote machine.However, you can omit the 127.0.0.1 since it will default to it if no other address is specified to create the flag:Shortest One-Liner (Using SSH Config)If you’ve set up your SSH config file with a host alias like this:# ~/.ssh/config HostName xxx.xxx.xxx.xx IdentityFile ~/.ssh/id_rsaYou can simplify the tunnel command to:ssh -L 8888:192.168.219.1:80 pi-1If you want to learn more, you can read here:To summarize, whenever you need to access an internal IP or web interface from a remote machine, SSH tunneling is a simple and secure option — especially when dealing with graphical interfaces like router dashboards.

item.title: MCP Servers for Agentic Dev Platforms
item.author: Juraj Karadža
item.content:encoded: For the past couple of months, the concept of MCP has been booming. MCP (Model Context Protocol) was developed and released by the end of 2024 by Anthropic. It allows AI agents and LLMs to interact with third-party APIs in order to work with real data and allows them to take actions on your behalf.In the case of a Redis MCP, as you will see further in the blog, it means that you can search through your database using natural language.Now, to utilize MCP and allow it to interact with other APIs, you will need an MCP server that exposes tools for your agents to use.The big question is how to run your MCP servers without making life harder for your developers. Having everyone set up their own local server is tedious and invites all kinds of inconsistencies. Centralizing it as a shared service keeps things simple, secure, and lets your team focus on building, not babysitting servers.Through the blog, we will cover how you can run MCP servers on Kubernetes. With such a setup, you can use them for different use cases and allow your developers to connect to them without a hassle.We know that Kubernetes can be difficult. That is why we created Cyclops, an open-source framework for building developer platforms on Kubernetes. Abstract the complexities of Kubernetes, and deploy and manage your applications through a customizable UI that you can fit to your needs.We’re developing Cyclops as an open-source project. If you’re keen to give it a try, here’s a quick start guide available on our . If you like what you see, consider showing your support by giving us a star ⭐MCP servers support two types of transport, or “how an AI agent communicates with the server” — stdin and SSE.Stdin servers are run as a binary, and the agent creates requests to the server through the standard input and receives the responses through the standard output. This works well locally, but in a company setup, having every developer run a server can get messy.On the other hand, the servers with SSE (Server-Sent Events) transport communicate via HTTP. Agents subscribe to the server on the SSE endpoint to receive responses, and then initiate requests via POST requests.With the SSE transport, the MCP server is centralized in a controlled environment and exposed as a shared service. This not only improves the developer experience by eliminating the need for individual setup but also reduces configuration inconsistencies and minimizes the risk of introducing security vulnerabilities from locally managed instances.MCP servers architecture setupWe created a setup on how you can deploy your favorite MCP servers to your Kubernetes cluster. After you are done deploying your MCPs, you will have a centralized place where you can easily manage them inside the cluster or deploy new ones.With Cyclops, we created a custom template that allows you to easily deploy MCP servers through a UI — even deploying a custom MCP server from a Docker image.In the image above is the architecture suggested earlier.A dedicated engineering team owning MCP servers can run and manage them through the Cyclops UI. The team can configure the MCP servers to communicate with existing third-party applications like Grafana and Redis.From there, all the other engineers can simply connect their Cursor to the exposed MCP server and allow it to make actions on their behalf.Below are the exact setups on how to try this out yourself! Let us know what you think!Run MCP servers on KubernetesTo run your MCP servers, there are a couple of prerequisites:a running Kubernetes cluster (if you don’t have a running cluster, you can create one with minikube).AI agent — we used Cursor in the tutorialYou can now install Cyclops into your Kubernetes cluster with the following command (comes with the UI for deploying MCP servers):kubectl apply -f <https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cyclops-ui/cyclops/v0.20.3/install/cyclops-install.yaml> && kubectl apply -f <https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cyclops-ui/cyclops/v0.20.3/install/demo-templates.yaml>It will create a new namespace called cyclops and deploy everything you need for your Cyclops instance to run.Now all that is left is to expose the Cyclops server outside the cluster:kubectl port-forward svc/cyclops-ui 3000:3000 -n cyclopsNow that your Cyclops is up, we can run a Redis instance inside our cluster along a Redis MCP connected to that instance.To run a Redis instance, go to Cyclops and click “Add module”. You can now select from a bunch of templates, from which you can choose the Redis template.You can now set authentication for your instance (will be used for connecting an MCP server to your instance):I set my password to my-secret-password - we will need it later when connecting our Redis MCP to it. I can now hit Deploy to run my Redis instance.You will now be redirected to the Module details page showing your Redis coming to life. Once your Redis instance is ready, you can create an MCP server and connect it to manage your instance.To run an MCP server, go to the Add Module screen and select the mcp-server template. In the Configure section, there will be a dropdown determining what kind of MCP server you need, which in this case is redis.To authenticate the MCP server to the Redis instance we just deployed, we need to pass it the host and the password:REDIS_HOST - host of your instance. Your instance should be accessible on {module name}-redis-master.{namespace}. The instance we deployed is called cache and is running in the default namespace - hence cache-redis-master.defaultREDIS_PORT - default port is 6379 which we didn’t changeREDIS_PWD - set in the auth previously. In our case, my-secret-password. For production use cases, you can use the secret references below in the UI.Once your MCP server is up and running you need to expose it outside of your cluster. Fort testing, you can do it by port-forwarding it. You can do that by running the following command:kubectl port-forward -n default svc/redis-mcp 8000:8000If you named your MCP module differently, replace redis-mcp with the name of your Module.You can now configure Cursor to connect to your MCP server in the JSON configuration:{ "mcpServers": { "url": "<http://localhost:8000/sse>" }You can now ask Cursor about the state of your Redis database and data inside it.With the template you just used, you can easily deploy a Wikipedia MCP server, a Grafana MCP server, or something custom by selecting custom in the service dropdown and defining the Docker image in the Custom Docker image override section.We believe that AI is finding its place in the developer workflow, but there should be levels of abstractions and validations to enable it and make it as safe as possible. This is our first step in this direction.We built this ; feel free to try it out and let us know what you think! Join our Discord and help shape the discussion around AI and Kubernetes 🙌

item.title: Mastering CI/CD with GitHub Actions: Connecting the Gears (Part 3)
item.author: Houssem Kraoua
item.content:encoded: So far, we’ve extended our assembly line in Part 2. The machines are running, the parts are moving — but now it’s time to add and to our factory.Think of this chapter as upgrading our workflow with smarter tracking, better communication between jobs and conditional switches that make our assembly line more responsive and efficient.🏷️ Implement versioning to uniquely identify our product.🔄 Use Outputs between jobs for seamless data flow.🧠 Use conditionals to control job execution.📦 Intro to the Delivery Process.First, let’s recap the layout of our workflow that we have been using:name: CI Workflowon: REGISTRY_URL: "docker.io" # Change it in case you have another registry IMAGE_NAME: "hello-world" # Change it in case you have a different repository name IMAGE_TAG: "v1.0" install-and-test: name: Install and Test - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 uses: actions/setup-node@v3 node-version: 20 - name: Install dependencies run: npm install run: npm test needs: install-and-test name: Build and Push Docker Image - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Log in to Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }} username: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }} password: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }} - name: Build Docker Image run: | docker build -t ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }}/${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ env.IMAGE_TAG }} . - name: Push Docker Image run: | docker push ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }}/${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ env.IMAGE_TAG }}Before we dive in, a quick heads-up about :While the job names used throughout our series (like install-and-test) help keep things simple and consistent, they may not always capture every responsibility that job performs.Feel free to rename and to fit your own projects and preferences. What matters most is understanding the logic and flow behind them, not the exact names.Now it’s time to connect the gears!🏷️ 1. Dynamic Versioning StrategiesIn any factory, it is important to identify every product at some point, whether it be a prototype for internal testing purposes or a finished one that customers will buy. For that, we are now going to look at how to implement two different versioning strategies in our workflow according to different requirements.Commit Hash Versioning (Recommended for testing and development purposesLet’s say we’re still in the prototyping phase(development & test) — just exploring our products, not yet shipping to our customers. In this case, we don’t need official release versions, we just need a fast and unique identification label for each product — something internal that will provide us traceability through every build.That’s where comes in.We’ll take the first 6 characters of the commit SHA (the unique ID of the code that triggered the workflow) and use it as our version — something like 3b8f2r. This way, we know exactly which code this build came from, without implying it’s a stable release.Now we can update our workflow (install-and-test ) to generate a version on the fly:jobs: install-and-test: name: Install and Test - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 uses: actions/setup-node@v3 node-version: 20 - name: Install dependencies run: npm install run: npm test - name: Set version using commit hash run: | VERSION="${GITHUB_SHA::6}" # Extract the first 6 characters of the last commit SHA echo "Build version: $VERSION"(Recommended for production and stable releases)In a real factory, labeling finished products isn’t random — it’s systematic.If we fix a bug, we bump the .If we upgrade a functionality, we raise the .If we break the existing model? That’s a jump.Basically the implementation of this strategy will be based on .Take user input for the version bump type. (major, minor, or patch)Fetch the latest Git tag from the used repository or use the default one(v0.0.0Increment it based on input.Push the new tag back to the GitHub Repo.All of this happens right inside the install-and-test as well:name: CI Workflowon: inputs: description: "Select version bump type" default: "patch" options: - minor REGISTRY_URL: "docker.io" IMAGE_NAME: "hello-world" install-and-test: name: Install and Test - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 uses: actions/setup-node@v3 node-version: 20 - name: Install dependencies run: npm install run: npm test - name: Set version using semantic run: | git config user.email "youremail@example.com" git config user.name "yourusername" git fetch --tags # Get the latest tag, or use a default(v0.0.0) if none exist latest_tag=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 2>/dev/null || echo "v0.0.0") echo "Latest tag: $latest_tag" # Remove 'v' prefix to get just the numbers (e.g. v1.2.3 → 1.2.3) latest_version=${latest_tag#v} # Split version into major, minor, patch(e.g version is 1.2.6 → major:1, minor:2, patch:6) IFS='.' read -r major minor patch <<< "$latest_version" # Increment version based on bump type from the input case "${{ github.event.inputs.version_type }}" in major=$((major + 1)) patch=0 minor) patch=0 patch) ;; # Construct the new version with 'v' prefix new_version="v$major.$minor.$patch" echo "New Version: $new_version" # Create and push the new tag git tag "$new_version" git push origin "$new_version"...While there are many more advanced and nuanced versioning strategies that can provide greater consistency and reliability, this chapter focuses on the two simplest and most widely applicable approaches: for rapid iteration during development, and for clear, meaningful releases in production.🔗 2. Connect Jobs Using OutputsNo station in any factory operates alone. The part gets shaped in one station, typically painted in one station, inspected in another station, and shipped in the last one. All the stations can depend on each other, essentially having to rely on informations to do their job well as our pipeline works the same way.In our case we don’t want the build to go figure out what is the version that was created on its own.Instead, we want the install-and-test to pass it over, clearly and directly and this is where exactly come in.Avoid re-running the same logic in every job.Reduce the chance of errors or mismatched data.Jobs can communicate more seamlessly.It’s just like making sure each part in a factory keeps its label as it moves along. No guessing!Let’s update our install-and-test to store the generated version (from the semantic step we set up earlier), and make it available for the build : jobs: install-and-test: name: Install and Test outputs: # Exposes specific varibales from this job for use in other jobs version: ${{ steps.set-version.outputs.version }} - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 uses: actions/setup-node@v3 node-version: 20 - name: Install dependencies run: npm install run: npm test - name: Set version using semantic id: set-version # Custom step ID to reference outputs later # Set Git config git config user.email "youremail@example.com" git config user.name "yourusername" git fetch --tags # Get the latest tag, or use a default(v0.0.0) if none exist latest_tag=$(git describe --tags --abbrev=0 2>/dev/null || echo "v0.0.0") echo "Latest tag: $latest_tag" # Remove 'v' prefix to get just the numbers (e.g. v1.2.3 → 1.2.3) latest_version=${latest_tag#v} # Split version into major, minor, patch(e.g version is 1.2.6 → major:1, minor:2, patch:6) IFS='.' read -r major minor patch <<< "$latest_version" # Increment version based on bump type from the input case "${{ github.event.inputs.version_type }}" in major=$((major + 1)) patch=0 minor) patch=0 patch) ;; # Construct the new version with 'v' prefix new_version="v$major.$minor.$patch" echo "New Version: $new_version" # Create and push the new tag git tag "$new_version" git push origin "$new_version" # Export the new version as an output echo "version=$new_version" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"Now the build can use that version: build: needs: install-and-test name: Build and Push Docker Image - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Log in to Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }} username: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }} password: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }} - name: Build Docker Image run: | docker build -t ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }}/${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ needs.install-and-test.outputs.version }} . - name: Push Docker Image run: | docker push ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }}/${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ needs.install-and-test.outputs.version }}What happened during the build now is that instead of using the image version as ${{ env.IMAGE_TAG }}, we replaced it with ${{ needs.install-and-test.outputs.version }}. This change allowed the version to dynamically reflect the output from the install-and-test , making the build process more flexible and consistent.To make this even more robust, we can define the full image path once using a job-level environment variable. Here’s how that looks:build: needs: install-and-test name: Build and Push Docker Image FULL_IMAGE_PATH: ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }}/${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ needs.install-and-test.outputs.version }} - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Log in to Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY_URL }} username: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_USERNAME }} password: ${{ secrets.REGISTRY_PASSWORD }} - name: Build Docker Image run: | docker build -t $FULL_IMAGE_PATH . - name: Push Docker Image run: | docker push $FULL_IMAGE_PATHThis small refactor improves readability and maintainability, especially as your workflow grows.⚙️ 3. Conditional ExecutionNot every product needs to go through every station. If it fails inspection, it does not get boxed. If nothing changed, it does not need to be repainted. If it is not meant to be shipped — then do not put it on the truck. This is what is all about. It’s like providing smart gates between jobs in your GitHub Actions workflow.Only do this if the last step worked.Skip this if we’re not on the main branch.No changes? No need to rerun.And many more that can be customised based on your requirements.Without these conditions, your pipeline risks:Wasting time and compute on builds that are already broken.Tagging a version that failed testing.Accidentally pushing broken images.Tag and Build If Install & Tests PassRight now, we always run the set-version in the install-and-test .But if our tests fail, why would we bother versioning our app?It’s like putting a label on a product that didn’t pass quality check.For that GitHub Actions lets us control each or runs using if statements.Step-Level Conditional Execution:We want to ensure that tests have passed setting the new version. Here’s the updated install-and-test to enforce this:jobs: install-and-test: name: Install and Test outputs: # Exposes specific varibales from this job for use in other jobs version: ${{ steps.set-version.outputs.version }} - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 uses: actions/setup-node@v3 node-version: 20 - name: Install dependencies run: npm install id: test # Custom step ID to reference outputs later - name: Set version using semantic id: set-version # Custom step ID to reference outputs later if: ${{ steps.test.outcome == 'success' }} # Only run the set-version step if the test step passed with success run: | ... (use the same code as before)Job-Level Conditional Execution:Now, If we want to make sure that the install-and-test has completed successfully running the build , we can add a simple condition to enforce this. In the build , it would look like this: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest name: Build and Push Docker Image needs: install-and-test if: ${{ needs.install-and-test.result == 'success' }} # Only run this Job if install-and-test succeeded steps: ... (use the same code as before)Now These gates prevent us from wasting time or pushing broken builds as they also protect our reputation as a factory that never ships defects.Bonus: Other Useful Conditionsif: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' – Execute only on main branch.if: github.event_name == 'push' – Execute only on push events.if: always() – run even if previous steps failed (useful for cleanup)📦 4. Intro to the Delivery ProcessEvery efficient factory has a shipping area — the place where finished products are carefully packaged, labeled, and loaded onto trucks for delivery. In the world of CI/CD, that “shipping area” corresponds to our phase.So far, we’ve been busy inside the factory: (compiling code, creating Docker images) (running tests, scanning images) (applying versioning strategies)Coordinating jobs with smart logic (using outputs and conditions)But there’s one important step left: actually the product.What Is Delivery in CI/CD?Delivery means taking our final build — a Docker image — and deploying it to a real target environment. This could be:A local Kubernetes clusterA cloud platform like AWS, Azure, or GCPOr even an on-premises bare-metal serverIn the next part of our series, we’ll:🚀 Extend our pipeline beyond the factory walls📦 Add a deployment job to deliver our Docker image🛡️ Explore environments, approvals, and rollback strategies🧭 Show how to keep our delivery process reliable even in complex scenariosSo far, we’ve built a solid, well-structured workflow that produces versioned, tested, and trusted products. But without delivery, those products never leave the factory.Get ready — the real delivery journey starts in the next chapter.

item.title: Software architecture 101 | Ep-08 | : How to choose between ACID and BASE compliance for your…
item.author: Nagarjun Gopalaswamy
item.content:encoded: Software architecture 101 | Ep-08 | : How to choose between ACID and BASE compliance for your database ? refers to a set of properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability) that guarantee reliable, correct, and safe execution of database transactions — ensuring data stays accurate and consistent even in cases of errors, crashes, or concurrent access.: A transaction is all or nothing.: Data always stays valid and follows rules.: Transactions don’t affect each other’s outcomes.: Once committed, data is saved — even after a crash.What is BASE compliance ? refers to a set of principles used in NoSQL and distributed databases that prioritize availability, scalability, and flexibility over immediate consistency. It allows systems to remain responsive and partition-tolerant by accepting instead of enforcing strict rules like ACID.: The system guarantees availability, even if parts are failing.: The data may change over time, even without input (due to eventual updates).: The system allows temporary inconsistency but guarantees that, given time, all replicas will become consistent.It embraces flexibility and performance over strict data accuracy at all times.How to choose between ACID and BASE compliance for the database ?When ACID Compliance is beneficial:Banking and financial systemsOrder processing (e-commerce, ticketing)Anything where data integrity is criticalWhen BASE compliance is beneficial:Logging or event trackingHigh-throughput systems where (like in NoSQL) is acceptable

item.title: What is DevOps Observability (Importance & Best Practices)
item.author: Sourojit Das
item.content:encoded: Observability in DevOps refers to understanding a system’s internal state by analyzing its external outputs. It involves collecting, analyzing, and leveraging data to monitor and understand a software application’s real-time performance and behavior.DevOps Observability helps teams:Quickly identify and resolve system issues.Monitor system performance and behavior in real time.Ensure systems are consistently available and performant.Minimize downtime with proactive alerts and diagnostics.Leverage data-driven insights for system improvements.Align teams with shared visibility into system operations.This guide explains DevOps Observability, its significance, phases, key components, best practices, and more.What is Observability in DevOps?Observability in DevOps helps teams understand what’s happening inside a complex system by looking at the data it generates, like logs, metrics, and traces. With observability, teams can keep an eye on applications in real time, catch issues as they come up, and get a clearer picture of how the system is behaving overall. This means faster troubleshooting and a more reliable system.Observability in DevOps not only helps detect issues but also enables teams to prevent potential problems by identifying patterns before they impact users. It promotes collaboration across development and operations teams by offering shared visibility into system health. For complex systems like microservices, observability enhances transparency by tracing requests across services and revealing dependencies.It also empowers automation, allowing teams to set up automated responses to incidents. Additionally, continuous feedback from observability helps development teams understand the impact of code changes, driving informed decisions and improving system performance over time.Phases of DevOps ObservabilityThe phases of DevOps observability are all about gradually improving how well we can see, understand, and control what’s going on in a system so that we can solve issues quickly and prevent them from happening in the first place. Here’s how each phase plays out: First, QAs gather data from the system. This includes capturing logs (which tell us about events and errors), metrics (like CPU usage and memory), and traces (which show the flow of requests across different services). This collected data is the starting point for getting a clear view of what’s happening.2. Aggregating and Storing Data: Once the data is gathered, it’s time to pull it all together in one place. Tools like the ELK Stack, Prometheus, or Grafana help centralize everything so that we can access and analyze data from multiple sources in real time. This makes it easier to spot connections and understand patterns.3. Analyzing and Visualizing Data: With data in a centralized location, we can analyze it and create visualizations. Dashboards and graphs make it easier to see trends, detect patterns, and notice potential issues quickly. These visuals give teams a snapshot of system health, so they can get insights at a glance.4. Setting Up Alerts and Responding to Incidents: At this point, we can set up alerts to notify us when something goes wrong or when certain conditions are met. By having alerts in place, teams are immediately informed of potential problems and can take action right away before they affect users.5. Optimizing and Continuously Improving: The final phase is about using all the insights gathered to keep improving the system. By monitoring continuously and creating feedback loops, teams can find ways to optimize performance and resilience, ultimately reducing downtime and boosting the user experience.DevOps Observability OpportunitiesObservability enhances service-level metrics. Companies see its worth-and expect to spend more on it.The observability market includes a wide range of categories, such as app performance monitoring, which according to Gartner will become a USD 6.8 billion market by the year 2024.As per the Enterprise Strategy Group’s State of Observability survey, worldwide IT leaders are convinced of the worth of observability. A full ninety percent of survey participants said they projected it to become the famous pillar of enterprise IT.Importance of DevOps ObservabilityModern cloud app environments are complicated, running across 100s or even 1000s of compute instances in multi systems with individual operations. With the progress of microservices adoption, numerous individual and isolated system elements make tracing the source of failure time-consuming and challenging.As more organizations adopt agile approaches, the frequency of deployments allows DevOps teams to speed up software delivery.Regular deployments in any system mean introducing high risk into the system.With attention to CI/CD, DevOps teams rely on response to debug and diagnose systems efficiently.Observability gives that feedback. Automation is a crucial element in DevOps. It allows teams to unite the right people with the right processes, take action with shared data, increase performance across the complete organization, and tie it to definite business outputs.Observability is a procedure of proficiently giving proper contexts to all types of data that the app environment yields so that it is simpler to audit the result repeatedly.It is based on exploring patterns and properties not defined in advance.Automation keeps observability information flowing. Observability allows DevOps teams to comprehend what’s happening across multi technologies and environments to find and resolve critical problems. It keeps systems reliable and efficient and clientele happy.What are the Key Components of Observability?These components are the key pillars of observability! They are:Metrics are a series of data that display a system’s performance. They are gathered over a definite period, weeks, days, or even months. Metrics deliver a constant, point-in-time impression of the system. This lets DevOps developers and teams spot particular trends as regards the system’s performance.A trace gives DevOps teams an outline of the system based on the transaction or request made in the system. Firstly, a request is prepared for the system, & after that, it records the flow of any request from 1 service to another.Benefits of DevOps ObservabilityHere are some of the key benefits of DevOps Observability:: Observability assists developers in discovering and mitigate issues faster, giving in-depth visibility that allows them to rapidly determine what has been modified in the system and debug or fix the issues.: Observability assists in examining system availability, network, user behavior, capacity, and other metrics to ensure the system performs as it has to. A system’s observability is extremely important to companies with regulatory or compliance necessities to protect sensitive data. Information requires to be linked to know-how the relationships between system elements and how they tie to your business.: Sprawling distributed systems sometimes makes it tough for developers to know what solutions are in production, whether app performance is robust, who owns a specific service, or whatever the system looked like before the latest deployment.: Observability also allows developers to see a request’s comprehensive journey, accompanied by relevant contextualized info about a specific problem, improving its performance.Implementing DevOps ObservabilityImplementing DevOps observability is all about ensuring you have clear visibility into your system’s performance and behavior. It’s like having a dashboard for your application, where you can easily spot issues, track performance, and ensure everything’s running smoothly.Integrate with Your Tests: BrowserStack works with popular testing frameworks like WebdriverIO, MochaJS, and TestNG. With Test Observability, you can get a detailed view of test runs, see which tests fail, and understand exactly why​.: Whether you’re testing on BrowserStack’s cloud or locally, you can monitor your test results and get insights into any issues with your builds and tests​.: Features like Timeline Debugging give you a visual history of test runs, so you can spot problems quickly by reviewing logs and videos​.: Get real-time alerts when tests fail and even report bugs directly to tools like Jira, making collaboration much smoother​.DevOps tools streamline the collaboration between development and operations teams, enabling faster and more efficient software delivery. These tools are essential for automating processes, managing infrastructure, and maintaining system reliability.BrowserStack integrates seamlessly with top DevOps tools like Jenkins, CircleCI, and TravisCI, enabling automated cross-browser testing within CI/CD pipelines for faster and more reliable deployments.BrowserStack Test Observability tool enhances DevOps observability by providing real-time insights into web applications’ performance across various browsers and devices. It helps teams monitor, debug, and optimize applications, ensuring system reliability and efficiency.Common Challenges in Observability with Solutions1: Lack of Appropriate ToolsWithout the right tools, it becomes difficult to properly observe actions within a system. Teams may struggle to collect accurate data, which can lead to inconsistent information and improper alerts.Invest in the right observability tools that allow you to capture logs, metrics, and traces accurately.Use tools like Prometheus for metrics, ELK Stack for logs, and Jaeger for tracing to ensure comprehensive data collection.2: Irregular Distribution of DataOften, IT organizations limit the understanding of observability systems to just the DevOps team. This leads to a siloed approach, where data isn’t shared evenly across the organization, making debugging more challenging.Ensure observability is integrated across all teams within the organization, not just DevOps.Share data insights regularly with development, QA, and operations teams to create a collaborative environment for debugging and problem-solving.3: Ineffective Alerting SystemDevelopers tend to set up symptom-based alerts, often overlooking the root causes of issues. This can result in an overload of alerts for minor errors, while ignoring the critical underlying causes.Focus on setting up cause-based alerts rather than symptom-based ones.Tailor alerts to identify the root causes of issues to avoid alert fatigue and ensure that the team can focus on addressing the actual problems.When collecting extensive logs, metrics, and traces, teams can become overwhelmed by the sheer volume of data. This can make it difficult to identify important signals and quickly address issues, leading to delays and inefficiencies.Implement data filtering and aggregation techniques to ensure that only relevant, high-priority data is captured and processed.Use machine learning or AI-driven analytics to help detect anomalies and patterns in the data, enabling more efficient detection of issues.5: Difficulty in Correlating Data Across SystemsIn complex distributed systems, it can be challenging to correlate data across various microservices or components. This makes it harder to get a clear picture of how an issue in one service affects others.Adopt a centralized observability platform that integrates logs, metrics, and traces from all services.Use distributed tracing (example, OpenTelemetry or Jaeger) to follow requests across microservices and visualize how each part of the system contributes to performance or failures.Best Practices in ObservabilitySome of the best practices for Observability in DevOps include:: Make sure all logs, metrics, and traces are collected in one place. This way, teams can easily access and analyze the data without jumping between different tools.: Focus on the most important metrics for your system’s health, like response times, error rates, and system resource usage. This helps you prioritize what to monitor.: With microservices, use distributed tracing to track requests across services. This makes it much easier to see where problems are coming from.: Don’t overload your team with alerts. Set thresholds that matter and focus on critical issues to avoid alert fatigue.: Automate monitoring and incident response wherever possible. This ensures faster issue detection and resolution, reducing downtime.: Regularly review your observability setup. As your system evolves, make sure your monitoring and alerting stay relevant and effective.Share Insights Across Teams: Observability data should be accessible to both dev and ops teams. This encourages collaboration and speeds up troubleshooting.Future of DevOps ObservabilityAn observability approach eradicates the potential threats of missing problems directly influencing app performance and creates an improved, complete experience.By 2025, 88–97 percent of seventeen diverse observability capacities are projected to be deployed. Very few respondents did not expect to employ these observability capacities (2–7%). This mentioned intent to employ a huge number of observability capacities is the most eye-opening result from this study as it put forward that most companies may have robust observability practices in place by 2025.Observability is still an evolving technology; few companies and professionals completely understand its importance. Many companies rely on a series of fragmented observability tools in DevOps to accomplish observability goals.Ultimately, shifting observability left along the Continuous Integration/ Continuous Deployment pipeline means potential SLO (service-level objective) deltas are caught before they reach production. DevOps teams looking to offer enhancement to app performance & business results can look to observability as a means to deliver both.

item.title: Inside AutoGen: Chapter 12 — Core | Multi-Agent Debate Pattern
item.author: Okan Yenigün
